title: Operators and comparisons

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Operators and comparisons; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapters 3-5: values, control flow, and data structures; atomic topic: Operators and comparisons
sources.claim: Python expressions follow documented precedence, short-circuit Boolean evaluation, rich comparison protocols, membership rules, and chained comparisons that evaluate a middle operand once.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Language Reference chapters 6 and 8: expressions and compound statements including match; atomic topic: Operators and comparisons
sources.claim: Python expressions follow documented precedence, short-circuit Boolean evaluation, rich comparison protocols, membership rules, and chained comparisons that evaluate a middle operand once.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Python expressions follow documented precedence, short-circuit Boolean evaluation, rich comparison protocols, membership rules, and chained comparisons that evaluate a middle operand once.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A range check uses 0 <= index < len(values), while two equal strings are compared with == rather than relying on interning identity. Your goal is to trace expressions and repair identity-versus-equality and precedence defects. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts, indentation and main guard; pf state expressions; Numbers, booleans, None and text. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python expressions follow documented precedence, short-circuit Boolean evaluation, rich comparison protocols, membership rules, and chained comparisons that evaluate a middle operand once. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: is tests identity rather than value, and/or return an operand rather than a forced bool, user-defined comparisons can return special values, and precedence does not replace clarity. The learner must reject this documented misconception: is is a faster spelling of == and and/or always return True or False.

includes: Evaluate arithmetic, Boolean, membership, identity and chained comparisons correctly.
Trace expressions and repair identity-versus-equality and precedence defects.
excludes: is tests identity rather than value, and/or return an operand rather than a forced bool, user-defined comparisons can return special values, and precedence does not replace clarity.

outcomes: Trace expressions and repair identity-versus-equality and precedence defects.

examples.title: Operators and comparisons: direct language case
examples.situation: A range check uses 0 <= index < len(values), while two equal strings are compared with == rather than relying on interning identity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A range check uses 0 <= index < len(values), while two equal strings are compared with == rather than relying on interning identity.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use == for value equality and is for singleton identity such as None; trace short-circuit expressions because and and or return selected operands.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use == for value equality and is for singleton identity such as None; trace short-circuit expressions because and and or return selected operands.

examples.title: Operators and comparisons: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A fallback expression uses name or 'guest' only when every falsey name should trigger the fallback; otherwise an explicit None test preserves empty text.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A fallback expression uses name or 'guest' only when every falsey name should trigger the fallback; otherwise an explicit None test preserves empty text.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: is tests identity rather than value, and/or return an operand rather than a forced bool, user-defined comparisons can return special values, and precedence does not replace clarity.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: is is a faster spelling of == and and/or always return True or False.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace expressions and repair identity-versus-equality and precedence defects.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: is is a faster spelling of == and and/or always return True or False.
misconceptions.correction: Use == for value equality and is for singleton identity such as None; trace short-circuit expressions because and and or return selected operands.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: is is a faster spelling of == and and/or always return True or False.

limitations: identity, truthiness, protocol dispatch, chained comparisons, and iterator behavior do not follow ordinary board-game rules

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace expressions and repair identity-versus-equality and precedence defects. Keep this boundary in view: is tests identity rather than value, and/or return an operand rather than a forced bool, user-defined comparisons can return special values, and precedence does not replace clarity.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Operators and comparisons using canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate terms and one observable consequence.
